critic_raw: The transformer vs. ARIMA stock price forecasting experiment has a fundamental design flaw: a stratified random split is used on financial time-series data. This is directly contradicted by the design's own acknowledgment that 'considerations regarding temporal autocorrelation remain.' Stock prices have the strongest possible temporal autocorrelation — tomorrow's price depends on today's price through both economic mechanisms and market microstructure. A random split that places observations from 2023 in both training and test sets while interspersed with observations from 2019 is an egregious violation of time-series evaluation standards. The ARIMA model is explicitly tuned 'for each stock' which is correct, but the transformer is presumably trained across stocks — this asymmetry means ARIMA benefits from stock-specific fitting while the transformer is generalized, which is an unfair comparison unless the transformer also receives stock-specific fine-tuning. MAE as primary metric for stock price forecasting rewards predicting yesterday's price (random walk baseline), which is a known pitfall in financial ML.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on financial time-series is a fundamental violation of temporal evaluation standards
Asymmetric comparison: ARIMA tuned per-stock, transformer presumably cross-stock — unfair unless both are evaluated under same fitting scope
MAE as primary metric for stock price prediction rewards naive random walk prediction

round_transcripts.critic: Three fundamental issues: (1) Random split on financial time-series destroys the temporal structure that both the hypothesis and the model rely upon — this is a textbook violation of time-series ML evaluation. (2) ARIMA tuned per-stock while transformer is cross-stock creates an asymmetric comparison. (3) MAE as primary metric rewards predicting the previous day's price — the random walk baseline may outperform both models on MAE.
round_transcripts.defender: On random split: the design explicitly acknowledges temporal autocorrelation concerns but then proceeds with stratified random split — this is an internal contradiction that is essentially indefensible for financial time-series. Autocorrelation in price series is not weak; it is structural. A rolling-window or expanding-window temporal split is required. On ARIMA vs. transformer scope: this is a valid fairness concern. The comparison should either (a) tune transformer per-stock too, or (b) tune ARIMA on the same multi-stock training set. On MAE and random walk: the design should include a naive random walk (today's price = yesterday's price) as a sanity-check baseline. A transformer that doesn't beat the random walk baseline is not useful regardless of its ARIMA comparison.

round_transcripts.critic: Defense concedes the random split is indefensible for financial time-series. The ARIMA vs. transformer scope asymmetry and the MAE/random-walk concern are also conceded. Three design flaws confirmed.
round_transcripts.defender: Concede: rolling/expanding window temporal split required; ARIMA and transformer must be evaluated under symmetric fitting scope; random walk sanity baseline required. These three corrections are necessary for any valid comparison.

round_transcripts.critic: Three confirmed fundamental flaws. The design as presented cannot produce valid results for the stated hypothesis.
round_transcripts.defender: Conceded. All three require correction before the hypothesis can be tested.

all_issues_adjudicated: Random split on financial time-series is indefensible — rolling/expanding window temporal split required
Asymmetric ARIMA (per-stock) vs. transformer (cross-stock) comparison is unfair
MAE as primary metric rewards naive random walk — random walk baseline required
